title: `uv lock` is not idempotent for impossible cross-variable markers
author: notatallshaw
body:
### Summary

I'm working on marker logic and doing a comparison of different libraries, while doing a scan of uv, I found a couple of bugs.

When two dependencies each carry an impossible platform marker, uv drops only one of them per lock, and which one it keeps changes between runs. `uv lock` never converges and `uv lock --check` always fails.

pyproject.toml:

```toml
[project]
name = "demo"
version = "0.1.0"
requires-python = ">=3.9"
dependencies = [
        "certifi ; os_name == 'nt' and sys_platform == 'linux'",
        "idna ; os_name == 'posix' and sys_platform == 'win32'",
]
```

Then:

```shell
$ uv lock
Using CPython 3.14.6
Resolved 2 packages in 181ms

$ uv lock
Using CPython 3.14.6
Resolved 2 packages in 296ms
Added certifi v2026.6.17
Removed idna v3.18

$ uv lock
Using CPython 3.14.6
Resolved 2 packages in 3ms
Removed certifi v2026.6.17
Added idna v3.18
```

`uv lock --check` always fails.

This is probably because `crates/uv-pep508/src/marker/algebra.rs` encodes known-impossible platform pairs in `exclusions()`. Its private conjunction writes results into the shared memo cache without applying the exclusion check, and the public `and()` reads that cache before reaching the exclusion check.

### Platform

Any

### Version

uv 0.11.28

### Python version

_No response_

title: !uv add in Jupyter notebook doesn't work as documented

It is necessary to use **Change Kernel** in the notebook and select **project**.

### Python version

Python 3.13.12

title: `uv pip compile`/`uv pip sync` silently ignore the `--cert` flag instead of erroring
author: goddtriffin
body:
### Summary

uv's `pip`-compatibility layer accepts pip's `--cert` flag on `uv pip compile` and `uv pip sync`, but silently ignores it — no warning, no error, no effect. This is inconsistent with every other unsupported/redundant compat flag (including the adjacent `--client-cert`, which correctly errors), and it's a silent footgun: a user migrating a `pip-compile --cert /corp/ca.pem` command reasonably believes their custom CA bundle is in effect when it is not.

### Reproduction

```console
$ echo "idna==3.6" > requirements.in
$ uv pip compile --cert /path/does-not-exist.pem requirements.in
# → proceeds normally; the (nonexistent!) cert path is never validated or used
```

Compare with the sibling flag, which does error:

```console
$ uv pip compile --client-cert /path/x.pem requirements.in
error: pip-compile's `--client-cert` is unsupported (uv doesn't support dedicated client certificates)
```

### Root cause

In `crates/uv-cli/src/compat.rs`, both `PipCompileCompatArgs` and `PipSyncCompatArgs` declare a `cert: Option<String>` field (so clap accepts `--cert`), but neither struct's `validate()` ever inspects `self.cert`. Every other field in those structs is handled with either a `warn_user!` or an `anyhow!` error.

### Expected behavior

Consistent with `--client-cert` and the module's documented contract ("return an error [when an argument] does not match uv's behavior"), `--cert` should error and point users at the supported mechanism — the `SSL_CERT_FILE` / `SSL_CERT_DIR` environment variables ([docs](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/concepts/authentication/certificates/)).

### Notes

This is scoped narrowly to the compat-layer no-op. It is _not_ a request for a first-class `--cert` flag or config key (that's #6715) and does not touch uv's global TLS behavior. I have a fix + integration tests ready and will open a PR.

### Platform / version

Reproduced on `main` (uv 0.11.28), macOS.

title: --extra from workspace root is rejected when using --package to target a member
author: Julfried
body:
## Summary

`--extra` defined on the workspace root is rejected when targeting a member with `--package`. The only workaround is `--all-packages`, which installs dependencies from **all** workspace members — not just the targeted one.

**Reproduction:**

```sh
# Setup workspace
uv init --name root --no-readme
uv add requests click
uv add --optional extra-deps httpx

uv init worker --name worker --no-readme
uv add --package worker requests rich
```

**`--package` fails on root extras:**

```sh
uv sync --locked --package worker --extra extra-deps --no-install-workspace --dry-run
```

```
error: Extra `extra-deps` is not defined in the project's `optional-dependencies` table
```

**`--all-packages` works but pulls in all members' dependencies:**

```sh
uv sync --locked --all-packages --extra extra-deps --no-install-workspace --dry-run
```

```
Would install 14 packages
 + anyio==4.14.1
 + certifi==2026.6.17
 + charset-normalizer==3.4.7
 + click==8.4.2       # root-only dep — should not be installed
 + h11==0.16.0
 + httpcore==1.0.9
 + httpx==0.28.1      # extra dep — correct
 + idna==3.18
 + markdown-it-py==4.2.0
 + mdurl==0.1.2
 + pygments==2.20.0
 + requests==2.34.2
 + rich==15.0.0       # worker dep — correct
 + urllib3==2.7.0
```

`--no-install-workspace` skips the workspace *packages* but still installs their *dependencies*. This means Docker builds for a single member with a root extra get an unnecessarily large venv, and there's no way to exclude sibling dependencies short of `--no-install-package` per sibling (which only skips the package, not its deps).

## Platform

Linux 6.17.0-35-generic x86_64 GNU/Linux

## Version

uv 0.11.28

## Python version

Python 3.13.12

title: Publishing package to Artifactory subfolder
author: ReyMagos
body:
### Question

Hello!
In my corporate infrastructure we are using Artifactory index to publish Python packages and each team has its own subfolder in the index to prevent collisions in package names. So I want to be able to deploy package directly to my team's own folder but it turned out to be not so obvious task.

Publishing directly to index URL:
`https://<ARTIFACTORY_BASE_URL>/artifactory/api/pypi/<INDEX_NAME>`
leads to an error that it is prohibited by policy. It's okay because Artifactory expects us to publish in a subfolder.

So we can try to add subfolder name in URL:
`https://<ARTIFACTORY_BASE_URL>/artifactory/api/pypi/<INDEX_NAME>/<SUBFOLDER_NAME>`
but then uv will report an 404 Not Found error. Indeed such endpoint doesn't exist in classic PyPI API.

Poetry 1.x was able to solve this problem by setting package name as `<SUBFOLDER_NAME>/<PACKAGE_NAME>`. But it is now not supported by PEP 508 as both Poetry 2.x and uv  use the standard [project] section.

There is one workaround from JFrog how-to (https://jfrog.com/help/r/artifactory-how-to-upload-a-pypi-package-to-artifactory-via-rest-api/artifactory-how-to-upload-a-pypi-package-to-artifactory-via-rest-api) that suggests using cURL to request Artifactory REST API instead of PyPI API. This works but I wonder if there is some solution to let it work with native uv publishing?

### Platform

_No response_

### Version

_No response_

title: Workspace discovery cache is rebuilt after settings resolution
author: dexhunter
body:
## Summary

A command can build the workspace discovery cache twice even when settings resolution leaves the cache root unchanged.

The early discovery performed while resolving settings populates a WorkspaceCache. run() then creates a new default WorkspaceCache before command dispatch, so the same large workspace is discovered again.

This appears to be a remaining in-process cache reuse opportunity after #12096 and the large-workspace startup work in #18311.

## Reproduction

I used an offline synthetic workspace with 1,500 members and measured a release build with two independent runs of 30 alternating baseline/candidate pairs.

Current main:

- workspace discovery builds: 2
- mean startup time: 185.775 ms
- median startup time: 184.607 ms

A small prototype reuses the early cache only when the provisional and resolved cache roots are equal:

- workspace discovery builds: 1
- mean startup time: 124.233 ms
- median startup time: 120.863 ms
- mean reduction: 33.13%
- faster in 59 of 60 paired samples

When uv.toml changes the cache root, the prototype creates a fresh cache and discovery remains 2 to 2.

## Proposed scope

- carry the early WorkspaceCache into command dispatch
- reuse it only when the resolved cache root matches
- retain existing invalidation behavior for workspace mutation paths
- add a focused regression test for matching and changed cache roots

The prototype passes the focused regression, uv-workspace tests, workspace integration tests, cargo check, clippy, fmt, and diff checks.

Would this small follow-up be welcome as a pull request?

title: Migrating a databricks project to use uv
author: yashovardhan99
body:
### Question

I know uv isn't fully supported in databricks yet (there is partial support with serverless computes but that's just for `uv pip install`). 

However, I kinda managed to migrate a legacy databricks project (think multiple messy requirements.txt with weird pins) to a proper pyproject.toml project with the help of uv. I basically cleaned the requirements.txt a bit, used `uv add -r requirements.txt` to create a `pyproject.toml` and `uv.lock` with proper dependencies.

The trouble starts when I want to use the new format in databricks. As I said before, databricks does not support uv (we are using databricks jobs which define `libraries: ...` to define what dependencies to use. For now, I found a way around this, I am basically exporting `uv.lock` to a much stricter `requirements.txt` with pinned versions. I am enforcing this with our CI process (pre-commit hooks to export, CI to validate by generating a new file and comparing). It works great, I can still use the `requirements.txt` in databricks, while ensuring I have proper versioning with uv. That is, until I run into the following issues:

1. `pip install requirements.txt` takes way longer compared to `uv sync`. On a fresh system, `uv` takes maybe 1:30 - 2 minutes while pip can take 5+ minutes to install the dependencies on the cluster.
2. Some dependency versions clash with the versions of the pre-installed libraries in the cluster (by default). This hasn't caused any issues per-se, but I have got a few warnings, and I am starting to wonder if we need to pin every dependency based on the databricks cluster.

My question is, is there any solution to this? Some better way to handle this mess? I wasn't going to ask this (I have seen the other open issues related to this topic), but I noticed the following tip in your documentation, so I thought no harm in asking:

> In general, we recommend against using both a uv.lock and a requirements.txt file. The uv.lock format is more powerful and includes features that cannot be expressed in requirements.txt. If you find yourself exporting a uv.lock file, consider opening an issue to discuss your use case.


### Platform

_No response_

### Version

_No response_

title: Migrating to uv for projects with sym-linked dependencies?
author: GregMilway
body:
### Question

I have been tasked with looking to migrate projects to a more modern package/project management setup.
Currently, we use `pip` and `pyenv-virtualenv` to manage our various repos.

The challenge I have, is that part of the dependencies are contained in a separate CI repo, that is then mapped into the various product repos via a sym-link, e.g.:

ci-tools
├─ python
│   ├─ .pre-commit-config.yaml
│   ├─ pyproject.toml
│   ├─ requirements.test.txt
│   └─ ...
└─ ...

some-project
├─ external -> ../ci-tools
├─ src
│   ├─ requirements.txt
│   ├─ requirements.dev.txt
│   └─ ...
└─ tests
│   └─ ...

It is set up in this way to keep the CI config common across all projects (there are more than 10 set up this way).

Would it be possible to keep the main and dev dependencies within each project, and keep the linting/formatting, and testing dependencies in the external repo, but still have uv be able to read and install from it?
If we did want to move to using uv, would each project need to maintain a complete copy of all dependencies?

### Platform

Linux 7.0.14-201.fc44.x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux

### Version

_No response_

title: Feature: Named, shared environments independent of a project directory
author: notatallshaw-gts
body:
### Summary

#1495 (conda-like global environments) was closed as completed by #18214; its duplicates #12740 and #13457 are closed, and #12358 (open) covers only listing. `centralized-project-envs` (#18214, uv 0.11.28) relocates a project's `.venv` into the cache, still one environment per project and activated by path. The named and shared use cases from those threads remain unaddressed and untracked.

Use cases not covered by `centralized-project-envs`:

- One environment shared across many projects or scripts, so heavy dependencies (e.g. PyTorch, several GB) install once rather than per project (#13457, #12740).
- Several named environments for one project whose dependency sets can't coexist (my case).
- One named environment shared across checkouts of the same repo (worktrees, parallel branches), reused without rebuilding (my case).
- Named ad-hoc environments not tied to any project, created and removed from anywhere, as with `conda create -n <name>` (#13457).
- Activate, list, and remove environments by name, backed by a registry and shell integration (#1495 companion requests, #12740, #12358).

Cache linking does not make this redundant. uv links cached packages into each venv (reflink or hard link, by platform and filesystem), but:

- across drives or filesystems (a separate data drive, NFS/NAS, or any cross-drive case on Windows), it falls back to full copies (`Failed to hardlink files; falling back to full copy`) (my cache does not sit on the same drive as my code)
- it dedups only same version packages, so projects resolving independently to different versions keep distinct copies, and the cache retains every version;
- it never covers per-venv bytecode (`__pycache__`) or the venv tree. It dedups files, not environments: still one venv and one activation per project.

`UV_PROJECT_ENVIRONMENT` can target an external env per directory, but offers no activation, listing, or removal by name. conda covers all of the above.

Without this, I stay on a conda + uv workflow and can't migrate to only uv.



### Example

Hypothetical command names to illustrate:.

```
uv venv --name app-stable --python 3.12
uv venv --name app-next --python 3.12
uv activate app-stable          # by name, from any directory
uv venv --list
uv venv --delete app-next
```

One env shared across checkouts:

```
cd ~/checkout-a && uv activate app-stable
cd ~/checkout-b && uv activate app-stable   # same env, no rebuild
```

title: `uv export` does not output correct relative paths for workspace members
author: noritada
body:
### Description

When a workspace contains two modules and there is a dependency between them, `uv export` does not resolve relative paths between their directories.
For example, when there are `lib` and `app` modules within a workspace and `app` depends on `lib`, running `uv export` in the `app` directory outputs `-e ./lib` instead of the expected `-e ../lib`.

(I think this behavior is unexpected, but is this intentional?)

### Reproducible procedures and its output

### Commands to reproduce

```bash
uv init --bare
uv init --lib lib
uv init app
cd app
uv add lib@../lib
cat pyproject.toml
uv export --format requirements.txt --no-emit-project --no-hashes
```

### `pyproject.toml` in the child package `app`

```toml
[project]
name = "app"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "Add your description here"
readme = "README.md"
requires-python = ">=3.13"
dependencies = [
    "lib",
]

[tool.uv.sources]
lib = { workspace = true }
```

### Resulting `requirements.txt`-format output

```
# This file was autogenerated by uv via the following command:
#    uv export --format requirements.txt --no-emit-project --no-hashes
-e ./lib
    # via app
```

### Notes

`sam build` runs commands as follows internally (which are executed from [`aws-lambda-builders`](https://github.com/aws/aws-lambda-builders), and fails with an error due to the issue above:

```bash
uv export --format requirements.txt --no-emit-project --no-hashes --no-default-groups --output-file /tmp/path/to/lock_requirements.txt --python 3.13
uv pip install -r /tmp/path/to/lock_requirements.txt --target /path/to/deps/dir --cache-dir
```

title: Add support for Pytorch cu132 backend
author: atom8be
body:
### Summary

uv is lagging on pytorch support, I noticed someone asking in 2025 too.  Please add support for cu132 to allow setting UV_PYTORCH_BACKEND to this value.
https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu132/
detecting a newly supported backend is easy, just check https://download.pytorch.org/whl/ 
relevant file: https://github.com/astral-sh/uv/blob/main/crates/uv-torch/src/backend.rs

title: Support for --package and --all-packages in `uv check`
author: sebadevo
body:
Hi, I work with a monorepo where I have multiple projects that we coordinate using workspaces from uv. Currently, to type check with ty I was first syncing my environment using `uv sync --all-packages --all-groups` and then running ty with `uvx ty check` and therefore ty was using the .venv containing all the dependencies.

When I wanted to use the pre-commit of ty at the root of the repo, I saw that the command --all-groups was available, but the --all-packages not.
For example, in my pre-commit file I have the following:
```yml  
- repo: https://github.com/astral-sh/ty-pre-commit
    rev: v0.0.56
    hooks:
      - id: ty
        args: [--all-groups, --all-packages]
```
which fails with the following error
```
ty.......................................................................Failed
- hook id: ty
- exit code: 2

  error: unexpected argument '--all-packages' found

    tip: a similar argument exists: '--reinstall-package'

  Usage: uv check --quiet... --ty-version <TY_VERSION> --all-groups --reinstall-package <REINSTALL_PACKAGE>

  For more information, try '--help'
```

I was therefore wandering if this was planned to be covered later or if there is another way to cover this (besides adding n ty invocation with --project for every project n in my repo).

Thanks in advance for all the great work

title: uv incorrectly excludes pre-releases of the base release of a `<V.post` specifier
author: notatallshaw
body:
### Summary

uv and packaging disagree on whether `0.12.a1` can satisfy `<0.12.post2`. packaging includes these as of 26.1 (pypa/packaging#1140).

The spec:

> The exclusive ordered comparison `<V` MUST NOT allow a pre-release of the specified version unless the specified version is itself a pre-release.

packaging reads "the specified version" of `<0.12.post2`  to be `0.12.post2` and therefore pre-releases of the specified version are of the form `0.12.post2.devN`.

<specifier>
Test Cases:
<details>
packaging >= 26.1:

```python
from packaging.specifiers import Specifier

assert Specifier("<0.12.0.post2").contains("0.12.0a1")
```

uv, fails on main:

```rust
#[test]
fn less_than_post_release_admits_base_prereleases() {
    let specifier: VersionSpecifier = "<0.12.0.post2".parse().unwrap();
    let range = Ranges::<Version>::from(specifier);
    assert!(range.contains(&"0.12.0a1".parse().unwrap()));
}
```
</details>
</summary>

In particular if you look at the property tests I add to packaging: https://github.com/pypa/packaging/pull/1144, uv's current behavior breaks the invariant:

> given versions v1 and v2 and v2 < v1 then if the specifier <V matches v1 it should also match v2.


References: pypa/packaging#1140, pypa/packaging#1144; #16868, #16881, #19778

### Platform

Any

### Version

0.11.28

### Python version

Any

title: uv 0.12 preview stabilization tracking issue
author: konstin
body:
This is tracking issue for which preview features we plan to stabilize in uv 0.12, the next breaking release.

### Stabilize

- init-project-flag: uv init --project changes from warning to error.
- project-directory-must-exist: invalid --project dir changes from warning to error.
- publish-require-normalized: non-normalized wheel and source dists are skipped from publish.
- python-install-default: uv python install adds python/python3 executables.
- special-conda-env-names: base/root conda env handling changes.
- target-workspace-discovery: uv run path/to/script.py discovers from target dir instead of CWD.
- toml-backwards-compatibility: rewrite pyproject.toml from TOML 1.1 to TOML 1.0
- venv-safe-clear: uv venv --clear on non-venv dirs changes from warning to error.

### Keep in preview

- adjust-ulimit: increase the open file limit on startup
- centralized-project-envs: default project env moves from in-project venv to centralized storage.
- detect-module-conflicts: complain when two packages contain overlapping files.
- metadata-json: built wheels gain WHEEL.json / METADATA.json and RECORD entries (draft PEP)
- native-auth: uv auth switches default credetial storage behavior.
- relocatable-envs-default: venvs become relocatable by default.
- tool-install-locks: tool installs/upgrades start using universal resolution and writing tool uv.lock.

### TBD

- packaged-init: uv init switches from unpackaged to packages layout by default.

title: Extras for dependencies break the sources resolution
author: joseparajelesGL
body:
### Summary

When using a dependency with optional dependencies, the entry on sources is ignored and the the index falls back to the default index
Ex.
```toml
[project]
name = "uv-bug"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "Add your description here"
readme = "README.md"
requires-python = ">=3.12.13"
dependencies = [
  # random dependencies for testing
  "coverage[toml]>=7.13.0",
  # dependency that needs the source entry
  "cuda-toolkit[cublas]==13.2.1; platform_machine == 'x86_64' and sys_platform == 'linux'"
]

[[tool.uv.index]]
url = "https://pkgs.dev.azure.com/<redacted>/_packaging/Common/pypi/simple/"
name = "private"

[[tool.uv.index]]
url = "https://pypi.org/simple"
name = "pypi"
explicit = true

[tool.uv.sources]
cuda-toolkit = [
  { index = "pypi" },
]
```
Generates this result
```
❯ uv lock
Using CPython 3.14.6 interpreter at: /opt/homebrew/opt/python@3.14/bin/python3.14
  × No solution found when resolving dependencies:
  ╰─▶ Because only nvidia-cublas{(platform_machine == 'aarch64' and sys_platform == 'linux') or (platform_machine == 'x86_64'
      and sys_platform == 'linux') or (platform_machine == 'AMD64' and sys_platform == 'win32')}==13.1.0.3 is available
      and cuda-toolkit[cublas]==13.2.1 depends on nvidia-cublas{(platform_machine == 'aarch64' and sys_platform == 'linux')
      or (platform_machine == 'x86_64' and sys_platform == 'linux') or (platform_machine == 'AMD64' and sys_platform ==
      'win32')}==13.4.0.1.*, we can conclude that cuda-toolkit[cublas]==13.2.1 cannot be used.
      And because your project depends on cuda-toolkit[cublas]==13.2.1, we can conclude that your project's requirements are
      unsatisfiable.
hint: `nvidia-cublas` was found on https://pkgs.dev.azure.com/<redacted>/_packaging/Common/pypi/simple/, but not at the requested version (nvidia-cublas==13.4.0.1.*). A compatible version may be available on a subsequent index (e.g., https://pkgs.dev.azure.com/<redacted>/_packaging/Common/pypi/simple/). By default, uv will only consider versions that are published on the first index that contains a given package, to avoid dependency confusion attacks. If all indexes are equally trusted, use `--index-strategy unsafe-best-match` to consider all versions from all indexes, regardless of the order in which they were defined.%  
```

However if I remove the extra it does work
```toml
[project]
name = "uv-bug"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "Add your description here"
readme = "README.md"
requires-python = ">=3.12.13"
dependencies = [
  # random dependencies for testing
  "coverage[toml]>=7.13.0",
  # dependency that needs the source entry
  "cuda-toolkit==13.2.1; platform_machine == 'x86_64' and sys_platform == 'linux'"
]

[[tool.uv.index]]
url = "https://pkgs.dev.azure.com/<redacted>/_packaging/Common/pypi/simple/"
name = "private"

[[tool.uv.index]]
url = "https://pypi.org/simple"
name = "pypi"
explicit = true

[tool.uv.sources]
cuda-toolkit = [
  { index = "pypi" },
]
```


```
❯ uv lock
Using CPython 3.14.6 interpreter at: /opt/homebrew/opt/python@3.14/bin/python3.14
Resolved 3 packages in 19ms
```



### Platform

Darwin 25.5.0 arm64

### Version

uv 0.11.22 (94988e5e0 2026-06-18 aarch64-apple-darwin)

### Python version

3.12.13

title: "Redundant" env markers removed in 0.11.25 break exported lockfile
author: bastimeyer
body:
### Summary

The changes introduced in #19933 have broken lockfile exporting. With `uv <0.11.25`, everything was working fine. Our project doesn't have a `tool.uv.environments` configuration.

**Here's what happened:**

Our CI tests need to export the lockfile into the requirements.txt format by running `uv export --frozen --all-extras --group test --format requirements-txt`. The reasons for this are irrelevant.

One of our transitive dependencies is `backports-zstd` (via `urllib3[zstd]`), which backports the zstd module of the stdlib on older Python versions and which is incompatible on Python 3.14 and above:

```toml
[[package]]
name = "urllib3"
version = "2.7.0"
source = { registry = "https://pypi.org/simple" }
sdist = { url = "https://files.pythonhosted.org/packages/53/0c/06f8b233b8fd13b9e5ee11424ef85419ba0d8ba0b3138bf360be2ff56953/urllib3-2.7.0.tar.gz", hash = "sha256:231e0ec3b63ceb14667c67be60f2f2c40a518cb38b03af60abc813da26505f4c", size = 433602, upload-time = "2026-05-07T16:13:18.596Z" }
wheels = [
    { url = "https://files.pythonhosted.org/packages/7f/3e/5db95bcf282c52709639744ca2a8b149baccf648e39c8cc87553df9eae0c/urllib3-2.7.0-py3-none-any.whl", hash = "sha256:9fb4c81ebbb1ce9531cce37674bbc6f1360472bc18ca9a553ede278ef7276897", size = 131087, upload-time = "2026-05-07T16:13:17.151Z" },
]

[package.optional-dependencies]
brotli = [
    { name = "brotli", marker = "platform_python_implementation == 'CPython'" },
    { name = "brotlicffi", marker = "platform_python_implementation != 'CPython'" },
]
zstd = [
    { name = "backports-zstd", marker = "python_full_version < '3.14'" },
]
```

Exporting this results in the `python_full_version < '3.14'` marker to be included, so when running on Python 3.14, this dependency will be skipped, as expected.

```
$ uv export --frozen --all-extras --group test --format requirements-txt | grep backports-zstd
backports-zstd==1.5.0 ; python_full_version < '3.14' \
```

However, now with #19933 merged and included in `uv >=0.11.25`, when updating the lockfile (e.g. by adding a new dependency and running `uv lock --no-upgrade`), the "redundant" markers will be removed:

```toml
[[package]]
name = "urllib3"
version = "2.7.0"
source = { registry = "https://pypi.org/simple" }
sdist = { url = "https://files.pythonhosted.org/packages/53/0c/06f8b233b8fd13b9e5ee11424ef85419ba0d8ba0b3138bf360be2ff56953/urllib3-2.7.0.tar.gz", hash = "sha256:231e0ec3b63ceb14667c67be60f2f2c40a518cb38b03af60abc813da26505f4c", size = 433602, upload-time = "2026-05-07T16:13:18.596Z" }
wheels = [
    { url = "https://files.pythonhosted.org/packages/7f/3e/5db95bcf282c52709639744ca2a8b149baccf648e39c8cc87553df9eae0c/urllib3-2.7.0-py3-none-any.whl", hash = "sha256:9fb4c81ebbb1ce9531cce37674bbc6f1360472bc18ca9a553ede278ef7276897", size = 131087, upload-time = "2026-05-07T16:13:17.151Z" },
]

[package.optional-dependencies]
brotli = [
    { name = "brotli", marker = "platform_python_implementation == 'CPython'" },
    { name = "brotlicffi", marker = "platform_python_implementation != 'CPython'" },
]
zstd = [
    { name = "backports-zstd" },
]
```

This results in `backports-zstd` losing its env marker in the exported requirements.txt format, and an env based on Python 3.14 will attempt to install it, which will not work and result in a build error.

```
$ uv export --frozen --all-extras --group test --format requirements-txt | grep backports-zstd
backports-zstd==1.5.0 \
```

### Platform

any

### Version

0.11.26

### Python version

3.14.6

title: uv export --format=pylock.toml omits the required packages key when there are no dependencies
author: atsuoishimoto
body:
### Summary

When a project has no dependencies, `uv export --format=pylock.toml` produces a `pylock.toml` that lacks the top-level `packages` key entirely. PEP 751 marks `packages` as required, so even with zero dependencies the output should include `packages = []`.

**Reproduction**

```console
$ uv init test-export
$ cd test-export
$ uv export --no-emit-project --format=pylock.toml
Using CPython 3.14.2
Resolved 1 package in 0.37ms
# This file was autogenerated by uv via the following command:
#    uv export --no-emit-project --format=pylock.toml
lock-version = "1.0"
created-by = "uv"
requires-python = ">=3.11"
```

No `packages` key is emitted.

Passing this file to pip fails, since the key is required:

```console
$ pip3 wheel -r pylock.toml
WARNING: Using pylock.toml as a requirements source is an experimental feature. It may be removed/changed in a future release without prior warning.
ERROR: Invalid pylock file 'pylock.toml': Missing required value in 'packages'
```

**Expected**

According to the [PEP 751](https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/pylock-toml/), packages is a required top-level key. uv export should always include it, emitting packages = [] when there are no dependencies, so that the generated file conforms to PEP 751.


**Version**

```console
$ uv --version
uv 0.11.26 (x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
```


### Platform

Ubuntu 24.04

### Version

uv 0.11.26 (x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)

### Python version

CPython 3.14.2

title: Warning when using uv_build from an in-tree backend
author: v--
body:
### Summary

I currently use `uv-build` indirectly from an in-tree build backend; see [`build_backend.py`](https://github.com/kcroker/dpsprep/blob/v2.8.1/src/helpers/build_backend.py). The configuration is as follows (copied from [`pyproject.toml`](https://github.com/kcroker/dpsprep/blob/v2.8.1/pyproject.toml#L95-L99)):

```console
[build-system]
requires = ["uv_build (>=0.10, <1)"]
build-backend = "helpers.build_backend"
backend-path = ["src"]

```

The frontend in uv 0.11.26 complains that the build backend is not `uv_build` ([link to code](https://github.com/astral-sh/uv/blob/0.11.26/crates/uv-build-frontend/src/lib.rs#L641-L645)):

```console
$ uv build
Building source distribution...
warning: `dpsprep` defines settings for `uv_build` in `tool.uv.build-backend`, but uses `helpers.build_backend` as build backend instead
...
```

As far as I could tell, there is no easy way to disable the warning, even though the code runs fine.

Can we disable this check for in-tree backends (e.g. in the presence of `backend-path`?) If not, can we add some option to acknowledge that the build backend is different on purpose? 

### Example

_No response_
